summary: Stancil argues that as AI makes it easy to capture and interpret messy, offline data (like conversations), companies will rapidly expand surveillance beyond clicks and use that data to build highly persuasive ad platforms. He connects this to AI’s growing power to generate software and influence people, warning that the incentives around free/flat‑fee AI services will push providers toward advertising and manipulation.
money_quotes: Companies don’t collect everything they can; they collect everything they can use—and that was clicks, not conversations.
As we find new applications for unstructured conversations, or phone calls, or video data, companies will find new ways to record them, and we’ll find new reasons to give it to them.
Chatbots could make really good advertisers.

If an AI company gives us enough free stuff—if OpenAI makes Jony Ive’s unimaginable technology free, provided that you leave ad trackers on—plenty of us will blindly accept its cookies as the price of a new generation of technology.
Every uninspired data company eventually puts out some marketing material that says their product provides “analytics at the speed of thought,” and everyone rolls their eyes at it.
key_insight: As AI makes previously unusable human data tractable and persuasive, the ad-driven surveillance model is poised to spread from clicks to conversations.
